content.assessment: International comparison indicates Hungary is already a strong outlier on early tracking intensity and between-school variance, and cross-national evidence shows early tracking is causally associated with higher inequality. A status-quo-with-monitoring approach retains the very features (85% of students in academically selecting schools, 71% between-school variance) that comparative evidence flags as exceptional and inequality-generating, without adopting any tested countermeasure from comparable systems.
opportunities: Monitoring data, if designed comparably to OECD/PISA metrics, could strengthen future cross-national benchmarking and inform which foreign mechanisms are most transferable
strengths: Monitoring aligns with the general comparative-education practice of building an evidence base before reform, which could support future context-appropriate transfer decisions
threats: Continued divergence from OECD-typical tracking ages and between-school variance levels may widen the gap between Hungary and comparator systems, reducing future transferability of incremental reforms tested elsewhere
Without structural change, Hungary remains an extreme case in cross-national terms, limiting the relevance of moderate international reform experiences as a guide
weaknesses: Preserves the specific structural feature (early, intensive tracking) that comparative data identifies as most divergent from OECD norms and most linked to inequality of opportunity
Does not engage with any of the tested reform mechanisms (e.g., delayed tracking) that have shown measurable outcome improvements elsewhere
